title: Kaitiakitanga o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku | Guardianship of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: English
culturalContext: Students will explore kaitiakitanga through the lens of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, a significant local waterway that flows through Ngāti Kahungunu rohe. The stream holds cultural importance as a traditional food gathering site and features in local pūrākau about the taniwha Mangakōtukutuku who protects its waters. Students will connect with the stream's historical significance while addressing contemporary environmental challenges affecting its health.
learningObjectives: Analyze and interpret traditional Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau about Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku to identify cultural values and kaitiakitanga principles
Create a persuasive written piece advocating for the protection of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using evidence from both mātauranga Māori and scientific sources
Collaboratively develop and present a kaitiakitanga action plan for the stream using appropriate oral language features and digital presentation tools
Critically evaluate different perspectives on environmental guardianship through structured discussions and reflective writing

activities.title: Pūrākau Analysis: The Story of Mangakōtukutuku
activities.description: Students read and analyze the traditional pūrākau of the taniwha Mangakōtukutuku, identifying key themes of protection, balance, and responsibility. They create visual story maps that trace the narrative structure and cultural values embedded in the story.

activities.title: Awa Health Investigation
activities.description: Students visit Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku to conduct water quality testing and environmental observations. They document their findings through descriptive writing, photography, and data recording, comparing current conditions with historical accounts from local kaumātua.

activities.title: Persuasive Writing: Voice of the Kaitiaki
activities.description: Using evidence from their research and cultural understanding, students craft persuasive letters or articles advocating for specific protection measures for the stream. They learn rhetorical strategies and incorporate both emotional appeals and factual evidence.

activities.title: Kaitiakitanga Action Plan Development
activities.description: In small groups, students develop practical action plans for stream restoration and protection. They create digital presentations that incorporate traditional knowledge, scientific data, and community engagement strategies, preparing to present to authentic audiences.

activities.title: Community Dialogue Circle
activities.description: Students participate in a structured dialogue session with local kaitiaki and environmental experts, practicing active listening and respectful questioning. They then write reflective pieces on how different perspectives inform their understanding of guardianship.

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua and local kaitiaki from the Mangakōtukutuku area
Hawke's Bay Regional Council's 'State of the Environment' reports and water quality data
The book 'Ngā Pūrākau o Ngāti Kahungunu' edited by Joseph Te Rito
Digital water testing kits and environmental monitoring equipment from the college's science department

tasks: Written persuasive piece advocating for stream protection (800-1000 words)
Group presentation of kaitiakitanga action plan using digital media
Reflective journal documenting learning journey and cultural connections

nzcAlignment: English: Processes and Strategies - Integrating sources of information, processes, and strategies to identify, form, and express ideas
English: Purposes and Audiences - Show an increasing understanding of how to shape texts for different purposes and audiences
English: Ideas - Select, develop, and communicate ideas on a range of topics
Social Sciences: Understand how people participate individually and collectively in response to community challenges
Health and Physical Education: Safety Management - Students will identify risks and their rights and responsibilities in relation to the environment
